Faith and prayer must go together, for the prayer of faith is an effective prayer. David gave up his soul to God in a special way. And with those words, our Lord Jesus yielded up his last breath on the cross, and made his soul a free-will offering for sin, laying down his life as a ransom. But David is here presented as a man in distress and trouble. And his great concern is about his soul, his spirit, his better part. Many think that while they are perplexed about their worldly affairs, and their cares multiply, they may be excused if they neglect their souls; but we should be even more concerned to look to our souls, so that, though the outward person perishes, the inward person may suffer no damage. The redemption of the soul is so precious that it would have ceased forever if Christ had not undertaken it. Having relied on God's mercy, he will be glad and rejoice in it. God looks upon our souls when we are in trouble to see whether they are humbled for sin and made better by the affliction. Every believer will meet with such dangers and deliverances until he is delivered from death, his last enemy.

David’s troubles made him a man of sorrows. In this way, he was a type of Christ, who was acquainted with grief. David acknowledged that his afflictions were deserved by his own sins, but Christ suffered for ours. David's friends dared not give him any assistance. Let us not think it strange if we are deserted in this way, but make sure of a Friend in heaven who will not fail. God will be sure to order and dispose all things for the best to those who commit their spirits also into his hand. The time of life is in God’s hands, to lengthen or shorten, make bitter or sweet, according to the counsel of his will. The way of man is not in himself, nor in our friends' hands, nor in our enemies' hands, but in God’s. In this faith and confidence he prays that the Lord would save him for his mercy’s sake, and not for any merit of his own. He prophesies the silencing of those who reproach and speak evil of the people of God. There is a day coming when the Lord will execute judgment upon them. In the meantime, we should engage ourselves by doing good, if possible, to silence the ignorance of foolish men.

Instead of yielding to impatience or despondency under our troubles, we should turn our thoughts to the goodness of the Lord towards those who fear and trust in Him. All comes to sinners through the wondrous gift of the only-begotten Son of God, to be an atonement for their sins. Let not any yield to unbelief, or think, under discouraging circumstances, that they are cut off from before the eyes of the Lord and left to the pride of men. Lord, pardon our complaints and fears; increase our faith, patience, love, and gratitude; teach us to rejoice in tribulation and in hope. The deliverance of Christ, with the destruction of his enemies, ought to strengthen and comfort the hearts of believers under all their afflictions here below, so that having suffered courageously with their Master, they may triumphantly enter into his joy and glory.
